model.label: Qwen 3.5 Plus
model.description: Alibaba • A balance between effectiveness, speed, and cost.

pick.rationale: Gonzaga holds a meaningful advantage as the 3-seed, ranking #11 nationally with an elite 9th-ranked defense that should contain BYU's high-powered offense while exploiting the Cougars' 88th-ranked defensive unit. Despite both teams dealing with key injuries—BYU missing second-leading scorer Richie Saunders to a season-ending ACL tear and Gonzaga without Braden Huff since January—the Bulldogs have Graham Ike healthy and leading the team, plus Mark Few's extensive tournament experience with nine Sweet 16 appearances since 2014. Gonzaga's 30-3 record, roughly 5-point adjusted efficiency margin advantage, and historical dominance over BYU (winning all four WCC tournament finals) provide a credible path to victory, though BYU's elite offense and AJ Dybantsa's star power keep this competitive enough to prevent higher confidence.

reasoningStep.title: Gonzaga vs BYU Round of 32 Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Gonzaga's elite defense, tournament experience, and historical rivalry dominance give them the edge over BYU despite both teams missing key scorers.
reasoningStep.evidence: Gonzaga ranks #11 nationally (KenPom/Torvik) with 9th-ranked defense vs BYU's 88th-ranked defense; Gonzaga 30-3 record vs BYU 23-11; Gonzaga leads all-time series winning all 4 WCC tournament finals; 3-seeds win ~60-65% of Round of 32 matchups vs 6-seeds; BYU missing Richie Saunders (ACL), Gonzaga missing Braden Huff but Graham Ike healthy; Gonzaga +28.16 adj EM vs BYU +23.28 adj EM

toolCalls.summary: Returned combined Torvik/KenPom ratings for 2 teams.

result.text: West Preview: Arizona Is Primed to Get Back to the Final Four - Hoops HQ HoopsHQ Seth Davis Conferences ACC Big East Big Ten Big 12 SEC Mid Majors Women's Bracketology & Bubble Watch Rankings Login Subscribe Your All-Access Pass to College Basketball HoopsHQ Login Subscribe HoopsHQ Seth Davis Conferences ACC Big East Big Ten Big 12 SEC Mid Majors Women's Rankings Transfer portal Recruiting Betting & Picks Bracketology & Bubble Watch NBA Draft News Long Reads Advertise Terms & Conditions Privacy Policy Bracketology NCAA Tournament 2026 West Region Preview: Arizona Looks Final Four Ready The Wildcats haven’t been past the Sweet Sixteen since 2014-15 and haven’t been to the Final Four since 2000-01. Is this the year that changes? Tristan Freeman 0 Gift This Article Published: March 16, 2026 1:36 am EDT Big 12 Player of the Year Jaden Bradley celebrating Arizona's conference tournament championship Getty Images The 2026 NCAA Tournament bracket has arrived, and here’s a breakdown of some of the key players, teams and storylines in the West Region. Fittingly, two of the best programs in the West are the Nos. 1 and 3 seeds. Be sure to check out our previews of the other three regions. East south Midwest Power Watch The All-America teams haven’t been announced yet, but there’s a good chance West Region teams will be well-represented. BYU 6-foot-9 freshman forward AJ Dybantsa leads the nation in scoring at 25.3 points per game and is the potential No. 1 NBA Draft pick in June. Another star freshman is Arkansas 6-foot-3 guard Darius Acuff, who led the SEC in scoring (22.7 points per game) and assists (6.4 per game). He’s the first SEC player to lead the league in both categories since Pete Maravich in 1969-70. Top-seeded Arizona is led by Big 12 Player of the Year Jaden Bradley, a 6-foot-3 senior guard who averages 13.3 points and 4.5 assists. Gonzaga has the West Coast Conference Player of the Year in Graham Ike, a 6-foot-9 senior forward who averages 19.7 points and 8.2 rebounds. And there’s a good case that the best overall player might be Purdue 6-foot senior guard Braden Smith (14.0 points and 9.0 assists per game), last season’s Big Ten Player of the Year who’s on the verge of setting the NCAA career assists record. The West has some serious star power, including BYU’s AJ Dybantsa, the potential No. 1 pick in the upcoming NBA Draft Getty Images Under the Radar While Acuff gets most of the attention at Arkansas, 6-foot-5 freshman guard Meleek Thomas (15.6 points per game) also has been stellar for the Razorbacks, including a 29-point outing in an OT win over Ole Miss in a SEC Tournament semifinal. No. 5 seed Wisconsin has one of the best backcourts in the nation, with 6-foot-3 senior Nick Boyd (20.6 points and 4.2 assists) and 6-foot-4 junior John Blackwell (19.0 points per game). Among the lower seeds, 30-win High Point has a star in 6-foot-6 senior guard Terry Anderson (16.0 points and 6.0 rebounds per game). And Texas has a fut

You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.

modelTrace.content:
Good information. Let me now analyze what I have:

**Gonzaga:**
- Ranked #11 nationally in both KenPom and Torvik
- 30-3 record
- Elite defense (9th nationally)
- Offense ranked 30th-37th
- Missing Braden Huff (17.8 PPG) since January with knee injury - unlikely to return for Round of 32
- Graham Ike is healthy and named to AP All-America Third Team - star forward leading the team
- Mark Few has extensive tournament experience (9 Sweet 16 appearances since 2014)
- Historically dominates BYU (won all 4 WCC tournament finals)

**BYU:**
- Ranked 23rd (KenPom) / 33rd (Torvik) nationally
- 23-11 record
- Elite offense (10th-16th nationally)
- Poor defense (88th nationally)
- Missing Richie Saunders (second-leading scorer) to season-ending ACL tear since February 12
- AJ Dybantsa (freshman phenom, Big 12 Freshman of the Year) is leading the team
- Beat Texas in Round of 64 despite Saunders injury
- Now in Big 12 (left WCC)

**Matchup Analysis:**
- Gonzaga's elite defense (9th) vs BYU's elite offense (10th-16th) - this is the key matchup
- Gonzaga's offense (30th-37th) vs BYU's poor defense (88th) - advantage Gonzaga
- Gonzaga has a ~5-point adjusted efficiency margin advantage (28.16 vs 23.28)
- Gonzaga is the 3-seed, BYU is the 6-seed
- Historical 3 vs 6 seed matchups in Round of 32 favor the 3-seed approximately 60-65% of the time
- Gonzaga has dominated the historical rivalry with BYU
- Both teams are missing key scorers, but Gonzaga's star (Graham Ike) is healthy while BYU lost their second-leading scorer


- Gonzaga's tournament pedigree and Mark Few's coaching experience provide a significant edge

Gonzaga appears to be the stronger team with superior defensive capabilities, coaching expertise, and a more favorable historical matchup. Their defensive strength could neutralize BYU's high-powered offense, while their own offensive efficiency should exploit BYU's defensive weaknesses.

modelTrace.content:
Good, I have enough information to make my pick. Let me synthesize everything:

**Key Factors:**

1. **Ratings Gap**: Gonzaga (#11) vs BYU (#23 KenPom / #33 Torvik) - Gonzaga has a clear quality advantage
2. **Defense vs Offense**: Gonzaga's elite defense (9th) should contain BYU's elite offense (10th-16th), while Gonzaga's offense (30th-37th) should exploit BYU's weak defense (88th)
3. **Adjusted Efficiency Margin**: Gonzaga 28.16 vs BYU 23.28 - roughly 5-point advantage to Gonzaga
4. **Injuries**: Both teams missing key players - BYU lost Richie Saunders (2nd leading scorer) to ACL in February; Gonzaga missing Braden Huff (17.8 PPG) since January, but Graham Ike is healthy and leading the team
5. **Historical Matchup**: Gonzaga has dominated the rivalry, winning all 4 WCC tournament finals against BYU
6. **Tournament Experience**: Mark Few has 9 Sweet 16 appearances since 2014; BYU has 0 Final Fours in program history
7. **Seed History**: 3-seeds vs 6-seeds in Round of 32 historically favor the 3-seed approximately 60-65% of the time
8. **Records**: Gonzaga 30-3 vs BYU 23-11 - Gonzaga had a significantly better season

**Upset Consideration:**
BYU does have a credible path - their elite offense could outscore Gonzaga if they shoot well, and AJ Dybantsa is a special talent who just broke Kevin Durant's freshman Big 12 tournament scoring record. They also proved they can win without Saunders by beating Texas. However, their poor defense is a major liability against a balanced Gonzaga team.

**My Pick**: Gonzaga
**Confidence**: Around 65-68% - This is a competitive matchup but Gonzaga's defense, tournament experience, and historical dominance over BYU give them the edge. Not a blowout confidence because BYU's offense is legitimate and they've shown they can win in the tournament without their second-leading scorer.
